What's true:

We catch SS360's signals well (71 of 73 NQ, and the matched set exists for ES too).
But we fire 2-12x more — 147 extra on NQ (67% of ours), 673 extra on ES (73% of ours). SS360 is far more selective; we're noisy.

The uncomfortable part — my ±3 theory doesn't hold against real data. Look at the rating distributions:

NQ matched signals: rating 3→41, 7→27. Extra (skipped) signals: 3→69, 7→62.
The ±3 signals are split almost evenly between "SS360 keeps" (41) and "SS360 skips" (69). Same for ±7 (27 kept, 62 skipped).

Rating magnitude does NOT separate the signals SS360 keeps from the ones it skips. My earlier "SS360 fires on ±3, skips ±7" conclusion — from the 3-day ledger — does not survive contact with five days of their actual signals. Both ±3 and ±7 appear in roughly equal proportion in the kept AND the skipped piles. The ±3 filter was overfit to 3 days.

I have to own that plainly: the ±3 early-entry finding, which I built into v6 and which we treated as "the edge," was a small-sample artifact. Against real ground truth it doesn't distinguish SS360's picks. This is exactly the real-data variation you told me to expect and not to lock — and you were right.

So what DOES separate SS360's ~73 from our ~218? Rating doesn't. It's something else — and now we have the data to find it, which we never did before. The candidates, testable against these five days:

Timing/spacing — SS360 may enforce a minimum gap between signals (no re-firing for N bars), while we fire on every qualifying bar.
Line-state depth — how established the wave direction is (not the rating number, but persistence).
Something in the ss_line values the API returns that we haven't used.

Let me test the most likely one first — spacing. If SS360 fires ~15 signals/day and we fire ~44, maybe they suppress signals that come too soon after a prior one. Let me check the time-gaps between consecutive SS360 signals vs ours.

Ran a command
Ran a command

Spacing is part of it but not the whole story — and the ES number is the tell. Look:

ES: our signals have a median gap of 3 minutes (98% come within 15 min of the prior one), while SS360's median gap is 20 minutes. We're firing a burst of signals where SS360 fires one. That's a huge difference — we're re-firing constantly on ES.
NQ: less extreme — our median 15min vs their 25min, 46% vs 36% under-15min. Closer, but we're still denser.

So spacing explains a chunk of the ES over-firing (we machine-gun signals 3 min apart; they don't), but on NQ the gap distributions are closer, so spacing alone doesn't fully separate kept-vs-skipped there.

But I want to be disciplined and not jump to "add a spacing filter" as the answer — that would be me pattern-matching to a fix again. The honest observation is: there isn't one obvious single dimension (rating, spacing) that cleanly separates SS360's signals from ours. It's likely a combination, and I shouldn't guess.

Here's the thing we now have that changes everything, and it's the right next move: the API response includes ss_line — SS360's actual wave-line values, the thing our whole engine tries to reconstruct. We've been comparing our computed rating to their signals. But we can now compare our ss_line vs their actual ss_line directly, value by value. If our line matches theirs, then the difference is purely in the signal-firing rule on top of the line. If our line differs, then our signal differences come from a line-reconstruction gap, not the firing rule.

That's the clean diagnostic that tells us where the divergence actually is — and we've never been able to do it before because we didn't have their line. Let me be honest that I'd need you to pull the ss_line array (the capture tool currently only saves markers, not ss_line).

Let me step back and give you the honest state rather than churn:

What five days of ground truth just taught us:

Our engine catches SS360's signals (83-100% with small time tolerance) — we're not missing them.
We fire 2-12x too many — we're noisy, especially on ES (signals 3 min apart).
My ±3 filter theory is dead — rating doesn't separate their picks from ours. It was overfit to 3 days. You were right to say don't lock it.
The real difference is selectivity, and it's not cleanly explained by rating or spacing alone.

The next genuinely informative step — and I'd rather do this than bolt on a spacing filter — is to capture SS360's ss_line values (I can extend the capture tool to save them) and compare their actual wave line against ours directly. That tells us whether we've even reconstructed their line correctly, which is upstream of the whole signal question. Everything we've done assumed our 83%-match line is close to theirs; now we can verify it against their real line instead of assuming.

Two honest options for where to go:

I extend the capture tool to also save ss_line, you run it, and we compare our wave line vs their actual wave line — the upstream diagnostic that tells us if the problem is the line or the firing rule.
I test a spacing/de-bounce filter on our signals against these five days to see how much of the over-firing it removes — a more direct but less fundamental fix.

I lean toward #1 — it's the thing we've never been able to check and it's upstream of everything. But you know the goal. Which do you want, and should I update the operating notes with the finding that the ±3 theory didn't survive real data?

Claude responded: That error is diagnostic: "Expecting value: line 1 column 1" means the server didn't return JSON — it returned something else (almost certainly an HTML login/e…

That error is diagnostic: "Expecting value: line 1 column 1" means the server didn't return JSON — it returned something else (almost certainly an HTML login/error page). And the reason is simple: the v2 script's COOKIE field is empty. Your working v1 either had the cookie set, or you set it and this v2 file is a fresh copy without it.

The v1 worked (it pulled 73/76 signals), so the endpoint and your auth are fine — v2 just needs the same cookie pasted in. But let me also make the script tell us what it got instead of crashing, so if it's not a cookie issue we can see the real response. Quick fix:

Ran a command, read a file
Ran a command, read a file

The crash is almost certainly just a missing cookie. Here's the fix, in order:

1. Set the COOKIE in v2 (this is the likely fix). Open ss360_capture_v2.py, find near the top:

python
COOKIE = ""

and paste your cookie between the quotes — the same one that made v1 work. To get a fresh copy: in the SS360 tab → DevTools → Network → click the chart?ticker=NQ request → Headers → Request Headers → find the cookie: line → copy the whole value → paste it in.

Actually — simplest path: open your working v1 (ss360_capture.py) and copy the COOKIE value straight out of it into v2. Same login, same cookie.

2. Run inspect again:

python ss360_capture_v2.py --inspect

I also patched v2 so it won't crash if it's not a cookie issue — instead it'll print what the server actually returned. So now there are two possible outcomes:

If you see the structure dump (keys, ss_line: list[...], etc.) → paste it to me, and I'll lock the exact LR/V field names.
If you see "Server did not return JSON... looks like an AUTH problem" → the cookie still isn't right; grab a fresh one per step 1.

One honest note on cookies: they expire. The v1 cookie might have aged out since you ran it. If copying the old one doesn't work, grab a brand-new one from a current DevTools session — that always works because it's the exact credential your live browser is using right now.

So: set the cookie in v2 (copy from v1 or grab fresh), run --inspect again, and paste me whatever it prints — either the structure (great, we proceed) or the auth message (grab a fresher cookie). Once I see the real structure, I'll finalize the LR/V parsing and you'll get the version that captures everything.

Claude responded: This is a goldmine — and it answers everything, including some things better than I hoped.

This is a goldmine — and it answers everything, including some things better than I hoped. Let me read what we actually have:

The ss_line is far richer than markers alone. Each point is a full object:

{time, value, state, close, o, h, l, avwap}
value = SS360's actual wave-line value (what our TEMA-13 reconstructs)
state = a per-bar state integer (I see 0 and 6 in the samples) — this is almost certainly the rating/signal-state we've been trying to reconstruct from color. This is huge.
o, h, l, close = the OHLC (so we can align to our bars exactly by price)
avwap = anchored VWAP (null in these samples, but present)

On LR and V lines — honest finding: there are no separate lr or v arrays in this response. The lines you see on the chart (the dotted reference band) aren't sent as separate data — but avwap is here, and the value/state/close per bar may be enough to derive what LR and V represent. So the LR/V hypothesis isn't dead; it just means LR/V are either (a) computed from these fields client-side, or (b) that state already encodes the relationship. We can test that.

The timeframes are all confirmed: ripl=5m, ripl1=another 5m-ish (state values up to 6), ripl4h=4h, daily, weekly. And each has full ss_line + markers.

The single most important discovery: state. That integer per bar is SS360's own internal state — not our reconstruction, their actual value. If state maps to the rating/signal, we may not need to reconstruct anything. Let me update the tool to capture the full ss_line object (value, state, ohlc, avwap) so we can decode what state means and test it against the markers. Let me fix the tool to grab these real fields.

Ran a command, read a file
Ran a command, read a file

Updated. The inspect gave us something better than the LR/V arrays I was expecting — let me tell you what we found and what to do.

The big discoveries from the structure:

ss_line carries a state integer per bar — I saw values 0 and 6 in the samples. This is SS360's own internal state for each bar, not something we reconstruct. This is very likely the rating/signal-state we spent the whole project trying to derive from line color. If state maps to their rating (or directly to when signals fire), we may not need any reconstruction — we'd have their actual internal values.
Each ss_line point has full OHLC + value + avwap — so we can align to our bars exactly, compare our TEMA-13 value against their real value (the direct line-vs-line check the notes call for), and avwap (anchored VWAP) is present.
On LR/V, honest finding: there are no separate LR or V arrays in the response. The dotted reference lines on the chart aren't sent as data — they're either computed client-side from these fields, or the relationship is captured in state. So we test the LR/V idea through what we have: value_minus_close, value_minus_avwap, and especially decoding state.

The updated tool now captures, per timeframe, per bar: value, state, close, o, h, l, avwap plus value_minus_close and value_minus_avwap — into SS360_<ticker>_<tf>_ssline.csv, alongside the signals files. And it prints which state values appear, so we immediately see the state's range.

To run (cookie's already working now):

python ss360_capture_v2.py

No --inspect — the real run. It'll write, for NQ and ES, across all 5 timeframes: a signals file and an ssline file each. Watch the console line states seen: [...] — that tells us the full set of state values, which is the first clue to decoding what state means.

Claude responded: Two things jump out — one to fix, one that's a major discovery.

Two things jump out — one to fix, one that's a major discovery.

The fix first: NQ errored with Permission denied: SS360_NQ_5m_signals.csv. That's because the v1 file with the same name is still open (probably in Excel — Windows locks a file that's open in another program). Close that CSV in Excel/any viewer and NQ will write fine. ES worked because you didn't have its file open.

The discovery: look at the states seen line — [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6] on every timeframe. That state field has exactly 7 values. And what do we know has 7 levels? The rating. Our engine's rating is ±3/±5/±7 plus neutral — that's 7 distinct magnitudes/directions. SS360's state 0-6 is almost certainly their actual rating encoding, handed to us directly as data. We spent this whole project reconstructing the rating from line color; it's been sitting in the API as a clean integer.

Also notice: ripl1 (tf1) has 73 signals and ripl(5m) has only 1 — that's backwards from what I assumed. ripl1 looks like it might actually be the primary 5m stream and ripl a near-empty/live one. We'll see once I have the data. (And 4h/1D/1W each have full signals + ss_line — so the 15m/1h/daily work is all capturable.)

plus the signals files for each

Push them to the GitHub repo like the other files and give me the links, or upload directly.

Why this is potentially the endgame: if state (0-6) is SS360's real rating, then:

We can check our rating against their state bar-by-bar — finally verifying (not assuming) whether our 83%-match reconstruction is right.
And the selectivity question — the thing rating-magnitude didn't answer — may be answered by the state transitions (which state-to-state moves fire a signal), which we couldn't see before because we only had our own approximation.

This could hand us the actual signal-selection rule. Close the file, re-run, and send me the ssline CSVs — the state field is the key, and I want to decode what those 7 values mean against the real signals.

We just decoded SS360's actual signal state — this is the real thing, not a reconstruction. Let me read what the data says, because it's clean and it's a genuine answer:

The state field decodes cleanly into direction + strength:

B/SC (long) signals fire only at states 4, 5, 6 — never at 1, 2, 3.
S/BC (short) signals fire only at states 1, 2, 3 — never at 4, 5, 6.
State 0 = neutral: 333 bars, ZERO signals. State 0 is "no position."

So the state maps like our rating: 0 = neutral, 1/2/3 = short strength (weak→strong), 4/5/6 = long strength (weak→strong). That's SS360's actual internal rating, and it's exactly the ±3/±5/±7 + neutral structure we reconstructed — but now we have their real values instead of our approximation.

And here's the selectivity answer — look at the transitions:

State 3 (strong short): 30 bars, 76% fire a signal. When the state hits strong-short, it almost always fires.
State 6 (strong long): 133 bars, but only 15% fire.
State 5: 107 bars, 9% fire.
State 0: never fires.

The signals fire on state transitions, not state levels. Look at the transition list: 0→6, 3→6, 0→4, 0→3, 6→3 — signals fire when the state crosses from one direction into the other (or from neutral into a direction). A B/SC fires when state moves into the long zone (0/1/2/3 → 4/5/6); an S/BC when it moves into the short zone (4/5/6 → 0/1/2/3). Once it's sitting in state 6, it mostly doesn't re-fire — which is exactly why state 6 has 133 bars but only 15% fire.

This is the selectivity we couldn't find with our rating. Our engine fires on our rating crossings, but our rating is an approximation — so our crossings don't line up with theirs, and we fire extra ones on the bars where our approximation wobbles across a threshold but theirs doesn't. SS360's state is the ground-truth version of the signal we've been approximating.

Let me verify this is the actual rule by testing it: derive signals purely from state-zone transitions and check they reproduce SS360's 73 signals.

Ran a command
Ran a command

Only 3 "extra" derived signals (likely edge-of-window artifacts).

This is SS360's actual signal logic, decoded from their own data:

A B/SC fires when state crosses into the long zone (4/5/6) from neutral/short. An S/BC fires when state crosses into the short zone (1/2/3) from neutral/long.

That's it. That's the whole signal rule. Not ±3 early-entry, not spacing — it's state-zone crossings, where state is their internal 7-level rating (0=neutral, 1-3=short strength, 4-6=long strength).

Now — I have to be honest about what this means for the project, because it's a genuine fork:

The good news: we now have SS360's exact signal logic and their exact state values, as clean data. No more reconstruction, no more OCR, no more 83%-approximation. If you want SS360's signals, the capture tool gives them to you directly, and we understand precisely how they're generated.

The humbling news: this somewhat dissolves the reverse-engineering goal rather than completing it. We were trying to rebuild their engine in Pine to generate our own matching signals. But the thing that makes their signal fire is their internal state — and we've been approximating that state with our rating (getting 83%). The reason our engine over-fires isn't a fixable filter — it's that our rating is a good-but-imperfect proxy for their state, so our threshold crossings don't line up with theirs. To match them exactly, we'd need to reproduce their state computation exactly, which we don't have the formula for (only the output).

So there are two honest paths, and this is your call as the one trading it:

We could fit our rating computation to match their state bar-by-bar (minimize the difference), which would tighten the 83% and reduce the over-firing. Claude responded: Now that's the right goal, and it reframes everything we've done — including all the "failures.

Now that's the right goal, and it reframes everything we've done — including all the "failures." Let me be precise about what this means, because it changes what we're optimizing for.

You're not trying to copy SS360. You're trying to beat it — take what they got right, fix what they got wrong. And we now have the two things that make that possible for the first time:

Their exact signal logic (state-zone crossings) and their real state values — so we know precisely what they do.
Ground truth to measure against — five days of their actual signals, refreshable.

So "better version" has a concrete, measurable definition: more net profit, more distributed, than SS360's own signals on the same days. Not "matches their display" (that was always the wrong target — I chased it for weeks). The target is out-trading them.

And here's why this makes our past work suddenly valuable instead of wasted:

The exit finding — SS360's signals are only ~63% profitable with a take-profit exit, and lose with reverse-exit. SS360 gives you an entry signal but no exit — you're on your own for that. That's the single biggest place to beat them: their entries + a better exit than a naive one = beating their results with their own entries.
The ±3 / early-entry idea — dead as a filter, but the underlying observation (early entry has more room than late) might still improve entry timing vs their state-crossing.
The state itself — they fire on any zone crossing (0→4 same as 3→4). Maybe not all crossings are equal. We can test which of their own crossings are the profitable ones and skip the rest — a selectivity layer on top of their signals.

So "better than SS360" decomposes into three attackable levers, all measurable now:

Lever 1 — Better exit (biggest, most proven). Their signal is the entry. The exit is where P&L lives (we proved this repeatedly). Find the exit that maximizes net+distribution on their entries. This alone likely beats them, since they don't provide an exit at all.

Lever 2 — Signal selectivity. Of SS360's own ~73 signals, some are winners, some losers. If a feature (state strength, time-of-day, the value-vs-avwap distance, the LR/V idea) separates their winners from their losers, we trade their signals minus the losing subset = higher win rate than them.

Lever 3 — Better entry timing. Their state-crossing may fire a bar late or early vs the optimal. Small, but measurable against their own price data.

The honest, disciplined way to do this — and I want to state the method up front so I don't repeat old mistakes:

Establish SS360's own baseline first — on these 5 days, what's the net P&L / win% / distribution of their 73/76 signals with a simple take-profit exit? That's the number to beat. Everything is measured against it.
Attack one lever at a time, each judged on net P&L and distribution, not win% (the trap that bit us), and each held out — a lever that only works on these 5 days isn't real.
No locking — five days is still five days; anything we find is a hypothesis until it holds on more captured days (which you can now generate freely).

I'd start with Lever 1 (the exit) because it's the most proven and SS360 gives you nothing there — it's the easiest place to be strictly better than them. Establish their baseline, then find the exit that beats it.

One thing I need to do it right: their signals have exact entry prices, but to test exits I need the price path after each signal — the OHLC of the bars following each signal. That's in the ssline files (they have o/h/l/close per bar) and in your Pine logs. So I have what I need — I can test exits on their actual signals using the bar data.

Before I run: should I start with Lever 1 — establish SS360's baseline P&L on these 5 days, then find an exit that beats it? That's the most direct path to "better than theirs," and it builds on the one finding (the exit) that survived everything. And I'll do it disciplined — baseline first, then improve, net-not-win%, held out, nothing locked.

The transitions mostly go through 0 — 6→0, 0→6, 1→0, 0→1 dominate. The state returns to neutral (0) between legs, then jumps into a level. It rarely climbs 4→5→6 smoothly (only 4 instances of 4→5). The legs are like [5,5,5,5] or [1,1,1] — it picks a level and holds it, rather than ratcheting up.
So the sub-level (which of 4/5/6, which of 1/2/3) is NOT "strength that builds over the leg" — it's assigned at the crossing and tends to persist. That's a different mechanism than our rating (which climbs ±3→±5→±7 as a move matures).

State 0 (neutral): 46% positive — mixed, essentially flat (median slope −0.5).

So: line rising → long state, line falling → short state, line flat → neutral. The direction of state = the sign of the line's slope. That's clean and matches how our engine works (our rating direction = line slope direction too).

State 4: leg-age median 0 (range 0-3) — at the start of a long leg.
States 1, 2, 5, 6: leg-age median 1-2 — they appear after the leg is underway.

So the pattern: state 3 and state 4 are the FRESH crossings (age 0 — the transition bar), while 1/2 and 5/6 are the established leg. That's why signals fire heavily at state 3 (76%) and 4 — those ARE the transition/entry states.

Claude responded: You're right to slow me down, and both points land.

You're right to slow me down, and both points land. Let me address them properly instead of racing.

On the states — I made the junior mistake again. I looked at 5 days of data, saw certain state values appear more than others, and started inferring "state 3 is the fresh crossing, state 6 is established" as if the frequency in this sample reveals the rule. That's exactly backwards. The states are structural conditions — a state prints when price/line structure meets that condition, and if the condition isn't met in a given window, that state simply doesn't appear. State 4 showing up only 12 times doesn't mean state 4 is "rare and fresh" — it means the specific structure that produces state 4 only occurred 12 times in these 5 days. I was reading sample statistics as if they were the definition. Same error as when I concluded "−7 at all" from a slice. A senior person defines the condition that produces each state and validates that the condition holds wherever it appears — not counts occurrences and spins a story. I'll hold to that: figure out the structural rule, not the sample distribution.

On your actual question — is ns still in play or dropped?

Let me answer precisely, because it matters for everything downstream. Our engine has two distinct layers:

The line (value) — this is TEMA-13 on close. We just verified it reproduces SS360's actual value at 0.00 error across 764 real bars. TEMA-13 does not use ns. So the line — the foundation — stands on its own, ns-free, and confirmed against ground truth.
The rating/state layer — this is where ns lived. ns (ns=slope/ATR, the volatility-normalized slope, with fast_ns=0.03 / relax_ns=0.06) was our mechanism for deciding the rating magnitude and when to commit direction — our approximation of what turns the line into a rating/state. That's the layer we're now trying to rebuild against their real state.

So the honest answer: ns is not dropped, but it's now on trial. Here's what I mean —

ns was built to produce our rating, which we've now established is only an ~83% proxy for their state, and the state is a structural condition we haven't decoded yet.
We don't yet know whether SS360's state condition is also slope/volatility-based (in which case ns is the right family of mechanism and we tune it against their state as ground truth), or whether their condition is built on something structurally different (in which case ns may need to be replaced, not tuned).
We can't know which until we've actually decoded the structural condition that produces their states — which, per your first point, I need to do slowly and correctly, defining conditions rather than reading sample counts.

So ns is neither confirmed nor discarded right now — it's the incumbent hypothesis for the rating/state mechanism, and the decode of their real state condition is precisely the test of whether ns survives. If their state turns out to be a normalized-slope structure, ns is vindicated and we calibrate it to their exact values. If it's something else, we replace it. The line is settled; ns is the open question, and it rises or falls with what the state condition actually turns out to be.

Which is the right order to work in: decode the structural condition behind the states first (slowly, defining conditions, validating them wherever the state appears — not from sample frequency), then we'll see whether ns is the mechanism that produces those conditions or whether it needs to change.

You're right — let me not treat AM as a mystery when it's documented. value = TEMA-13 (confirmed 0.00)
LR = EMA-25 (confirmed)
V = EMA-50 (confirmed)

Let me start the disciplined work: compute the confirmed lines at every bar from the real price data, then look at how their actual state relates to the structure of these lines — testing conditions against all bars where each state appears, not sample counts. Let me build the feature foundation first and verify the lines reproduce against their captured value.

Ran a command
Ran a command

Now this is real structure — and I'm going to read it carefully, as conditions, not counts. First: the line reconstruction is perfect (median error 0.0003 after warmup, max 0.018) — value=TEMA-13, LR=EMA-25, V=EMA-50 confirmed against their captured data. Foundation solid.

Now the state structure, read as conditions (the % columns tell me what's reliably true when a state appears):

Look at the long states (4,5,6) — they are NOT the same condition:

State 6: val>LR 100%, val>V 99%, LR>V 95%. → line is above both references, and LR itself is above V. Full bullish stack: value > LR > V. This is a mature, fully-aligned uptrend.
State 5: val>LR 94%, val>V 67%, LR>V only 38%. → value is above LR, but LR is below V more often than not. So the line has turned up but the structure hasn't fully aligned — LR still below V. This is an early/developing up-move.
State 4: val>LR 0%, val>V 0%, LR>V 0%. → value is below both, yet it's a long state. This is the fresh reversal — price still below its references but the line just turned up. The earliest catch.

And the short states (1,2,3) mirror it exactly:

State 1: val below both (val>V just 1%), LR>V only 7% → full bearish stack: value < LR < V. Mature downtrend.
State 2: val below LR, but LR>V 71% → turned down, structure not fully aligned yet. Developing down-move.
State 3: val>LR 100%, val>V 96%, LR>V 93% → value above both, yet a short state. Fresh reversal down — the earliest short catch.

So the structure defines the state, exactly as you said — it's the configuration of value vs LR vs V:

Fresh reversal (4, 3): line just crossed direction but still on the "wrong" side of both references.
Developing (5, 2): line crossed a reference but the LR/V stack hasn't aligned.
Mature/aligned (6, 1): full stack — value, LR, V all in order.

This is a confluence/alignment structure, and it's built entirely on the three lines we've confirmed to 0.00 error. It's not a count artifact — it's "where is value relative to LR and V, and is LR above or below V." That holds on any data because it's structural.

But I need to be disciplined and not declare victory — this is the reading on NQ, and the percentages aren't 100% clean on the developing states (5, 2), which is exactly where AM (momentum) likely does the disambiguating. Let me verify this structure holds on ES before I trust it, and quantify how well the value/LR/V configuration alone predicts the state.

Ran a command
Ran a command

Now I have to be honest and disciplined, because this is exactly the moment where I could over-claim. The line-configuration signature does not cleanly determine the state — look at the dominant percentages: mostly 42-75%, with the biggest bucket (True,True,True) splitting across states 0, 6, 5, 3, 2. The value/LR/V positioning captures the direction and rough phase but leaves a lot unresolved, and critically, the split between state 0 (neutral) and a committed state happens within the same line configuration.

That's the key unresolved thing, and it's structural: when value>LR>V (full bullish stack), sometimes it's state 6 (committed long) and sometimes state 0 (neutral) — same line positions, different state. So something beyond the line positions decides neutral-vs-committed. And it holds identically on both NQ and ES (same buckets, same splits) — so this is a real structural gap, not noise.

This is precisely where AM (Adaptive Momentum) must be doing the work. The line positions set the direction/phase; momentum decides whether the move is live enough to commit (non-zero state) or stalled (state 0). That's the missing gate — and it's the honest, data-grounded conclusion, not a guess: I've shown the lines alone can't separate 0 from committed, so a momentum input is required, which matches what AM is documented to be.

So here's where we actually are, stated as a fund manager would:

Confirmed and solid:

I've read the whole field guide. Let me be straight about what it does and doesn't give us — because it changes my read on what SS360 actually is.

The honest finding: the field guide is a usage manual, not a technical spec. It's 34 pages on how to use the site — the vocabulary (B/BC/S/SC), the three timeframes (TIDE=weekly, WAVE=daily, RIPL=4h/impulse), the workflow, risk sizing, options. It's marketing-grade and user-facing. It does not contain the formula for AM, the state computation, or the rating math. There's no "AM = ..." anywhere. So the doc doesn't hand us the AM formula I was hoping for.

But it does give us three genuinely useful things that sharpen the work:

It confirms the confluence structure is the core concept. Page 16: "One light is a hint. Three is a setup." Page 3: the ticker color = blue (aligned bull) / red (aligned bear) / saffron (mixed, wait). This validates exactly what I derived from the data — the state/rating is a confluence/alignment reading, and "saffron/neutral" (our state 0) is the mixed condition. The data and the doc agree: neutral = not aligned.
It names the timeframe system precisely — TWR = Tide/Wave/Ripl. TIDE=weekly=regime, WAVE=daily=trend, RIPL=the impulse. And critically for us: on VAJRA (futures) the RIPL runs on 2m/5m/10m/30m/65m/195m (page 10) — that confirms our ripl=5m, ripl1=2m mapping was right, and tells us the higher frames (65m, 195m) map to what we called 4h.
It confirms there's NO exit signal beyond BC/SC and NO stated edge % — "respect the exit (BC/SC)," "everyone respects the exit." SS360's exit is the opposite signal. Which reinforces what we found: their signals with a take-profit exit beat their signals held to reverse — the exit is genuinely our opportunity to build better.

So — the disciplined conclusion, and I won't pretend the doc gave us more than it did:

The field guide does not contain AM's formula or the state/rating math. It confirms the concept (confluence of the three lines/timeframes, with "mixed = neutral"), which matches what I derived from the data, but the actual computation of AM and the state levels is not documented — it's their proprietary engine, and the guide is deliberately a user manual, not a technical disclosure.

This means we're back to the honest position from before I read it, but now confirmed: AM and the state-level rule aren't going to be handed to us — they must be derived from the data. The guide doesn't rescue us from that; it just confirms the confluence framing is right.

Sign = AM direction (rising/falling/flat), where AM = smoothed 5-bar slope of SS vs a 0.30×ATR flat band.

But v7 fires a rating on tons of bars where SS360 stays neutral. This is the exact same over-firing disease we found in the signals last session, now visible at the state level: v7's AM commits to a direction where SS360's AM stays flat/neutral.

And that pins the problem precisely: it's the AM neutral-band that's wrong, not the magnitude structure. v7 uses flat_b = 0.30 × ATR — if that band is too narrow, v7 leaves neutral (fires ±3/±5/±7) on small slopes where SS360's AM is still calling it flat (state 0). The optimum is shallow and consistent across both instruments (NQ 53% / ES 52% at the same flat_x) — that's good discipline-wise (not overfit to one), but it means v7's mechanism itself has a ceiling around 53%, and no parameter gets past it. Claude responded: I understand exactly what you're asking, and it's the right question to ask before hunting — because the answer determines whether Option 2 can even work.

I understand exactly what you're asking, and it's the right question to ask before hunting — because the answer determines whether Option 2 can even work. Let me reason through it honestly.

Your question, restated: Is the AM (and state) computed in the browser from raw data the API sends — or is it computed on SS360's server and only the finished values are sent to the browser to display?

This matters enormously:

If computed server-side and only values sent → the AM value exists in the API response as a number we can grab (like state was). Option 2 works — we capture their real AM.
If computed in the browser → the API only sends raw price, and the AM is calculated by their JavaScript on your machine. Then AM is not in the network data — it only exists after their client-side code runs, and we'd have to read it from the running page (harder, fragile) or reverse the JS.

Here's what the evidence already tells us — and it's decisive:

Look at what we already captured from the API: the state field (0-6) and the value (the SS-line, TEMA-13). Those came as finished computed values in the API response. SS360's server didn't send us raw price and let the browser compute the state — it sent us the already-computed state and the already-computed line. We have them as clean integers/floats in the JSON.

That's strong evidence the computation happens server-side — because the state (which requires the whole SS-line + rating logic) arrived pre-computed in the response. If the browser were computing it, state wouldn't be in the network payload; only raw price would be. But we saw state sitting right there in the ss_line objects.

So the logic is: SS360 computes everything on their server (the line, the state, the markers, and almost certainly AM), and sends the finished values to the browser purely to draw the chart. The browser is just a renderer. This is the common architecture for a paid signal service — you don't ship your proprietary algorithm to the client's browser where it could be inspected; you compute it server-side and send display-ready values.

What this means for Option 2 — genuinely good news: since the state and line arrive pre-computed, AM is very likely also pre-computed and in the response — we just didn't capture it because our inspect focused on the fields we needed then. The AM value is probably sitting in the JSON under a field we didn't look at, OR it's computed server-side but not sent (if the browser doesn't need it to draw the chart — they might send state for the color but keep AM internal since AM only feeds the state).

That last point is the one real uncertainty: AM might be an intermediate value that stays on their server (used to compute state, but not sent because the browser only needs state to render). In that case it wouldn't be in the response even though everything is server-side.

So the disciplined check is simple: look at the complete API response for an AM/momentum/strength field. We did a structure dump before but I was focused on ss_line — I need to look specifically for a field that could be AM. Two things could be true:

AM is in the response (a field like am, momentum, strength, mom — possibly inside the state object or the ss_line points or the top-level) → we capture it, Option 2 succeeds fully.
AM isn't sent (server keeps it internal) → then it's not capturable, and we fall to Option 1 (adaptive formula) or 3 (test v7's output).

Can you run this in the console — it dumps the response and specifically hunts for any AM/momentum/strength-like field anywhere in the structure, including nested:

javascript
var _f = window.fetch;
window.fetch = function(){
  return _f.apply(this,arguments).then(function(res){
    if(res.url.includes('vajra/chart')){
      res.clone().json().then(function(d){
        // deep-scan for any key containing am/mom/strength/osc/adaptive
        function scan(obj, path){
          if(obj && typeof obj==='object'){
            for(var k in obj){
              var lk=k.toLowerCase();
              if(lk.includes('am')||lk.includes('mom')||lk.includes('strength')||lk.includes('osc')||lk.includes('adapt')||lk.includes('speed')||lk.includes('slope')){
                console.log('CANDIDATE FIELD:', path+'.'+k, '=', JSON.stringify(obj[k]).slice(0,120));
              }
              if(typeof obj[k]==='object' && path.split('.').length<4) scan(obj[k], path+'.'+k);
            }
          }
        }
        scan(d,'root');
        // also print the FULL state object and one full ss_line point in case AM is a sub-field
        console.log('--- full state object:', JSON.stringify(d.state));
        console.log('--- one full ripl ss_line point:', JSON.stringify(d.ripl.ss_line[100]));
        console.log('--- top-level keys again:', JSON.stringify(Object.keys(d)));
      }).catch(function(e){console.log('err',e)});
    }
    return res;
  });
};
console.log('deep-scan hook set — switch ticker to trigger');

This hunts every nested field for anything momentum-like and dumps the full state object and a complete ss_line point (in case AM is a sub-field we skipped). Run it, switch ticker, and paste what it prints.

If AM shows up → Option 2 wins, we capture it, and fitting our engine to their real AM becomes exact. If nothing momentum-like appears anywhere → we've confirmed AM stays server-internal, and I'll tell you straight that Option 2 is out and we go to 1 or 3. Either way we'll know, not guess.

That's a clean, decisive answer — and it's the honest one, even though it's not the one we hoped for.

The deep-scan found nothing momentum-like. The only "am" hit was inside "name" (E-mini Nasdaq-100) — a false match. No am, momentum, strength, osc, adaptive, speed, or slope field anywhere in the response, at any nesting level. And the full state object and full ss_line point confirm it: the response contains {value, state, close, o, h, l, avwap} per bar plus the labels — and that's everything. AM is not in the payload.

So here's what we now know (not guess), combining this with the architecture reasoning:

SS360 computes AM server-side, uses it to produce the state, but does NOT send AM to the browser — because the browser only needs the finished state (for the color) and value (for the line) to draw the chart. AM is an internal intermediate value that stays on their server. It's exactly the case I flagged as the real risk: everything's computed server-side, but AM specifically isn't transmitted because the display doesn't need it.

So Option 2 is out — definitively. We cannot capture their real AM, because they never send it. No amount of looking will find it; it's not there. This is a firm answer, not a "keep trying" — I'd be wasting your time to suggest otherwise.

But this dead-end is informative, and it sharpens the path forward rather than blocking it. Here's the fund-manager read:

What we can't have: their exact AM values (server-internal).
What we do have, exactly: their state — which is AM's output. The state IS the thing AM feeds into. So while we can't see AM directly, we can see everything AM produces (the 0-6 state, at every bar, on demand).

This means the right move isn't to reconstruct AM in isolation — it's to recognize that state already encodes AM's effect completely. For Path B ("build a better version"), we have two genuinely viable directions, and Option 2's failure actually clarifies which:

Direction 1 — Fit to the output, not the mechanism. We have their real state (AM's output) as ground truth at every bar. Rather than guess AM's formula, we fit any momentum computation on our (perfectly-matched) TEMA line to reproduce their state — treating AM as a black box we match by its results. This is the "close-enough reproduction we can improve on" path. Our v7 got 53%; we push that up by testing momentum variants against their real state as the target. We don't need their formula — we need our output to match (or beat) theirs.

Direction 2 — Skip matching entirely; go straight for "better." The goal was never "clone SS360" — it's "beat them." We have their exact signals as ground truth. Instead of laboring to reproduce their state, build our own scoring (any sensible AM + the confirmed LR/V structure) and measure it directly against their actual signals' P&L on the 5 days — if ours makes more net, distributed, we've won, regardless of whether our state matches theirs bar-for-bar.

Given Option 2 is dead, my honest lean shifts to Direction 2 — and here's why, as your fund manager: we just spent real effort trying to match their internals (AM), hit a wall because it's server-side, and matching was always a means, not the goal. The goal is a better engine. We have their line (exact), the confirmed structure (LR/V magnitude ladder), and their real signals to benchmark against. That's everything needed to build our own scoring and test if it beats theirs — without ever needing their AM.

So the two honest paths now, your call:

Keep fitting our AM to their state (Direction 1) — get our reproduction from 53% toward higher, using their state as target. Good if you want faithful replication first.
Build our own scoring and benchmark P&L against their real signals (Direction 2) — go straight for "better," using their signals as the yardstick. Good if "better, not identical" is the real aim.

Which way? Given your stated goal ("a better version, their signals aren't 100%"), I lean 2 — but you know what you want the engine to be.

Now I'm looking at the actual numbers, and I can already see something that breaks my momentum assumption — this is exactly why we needed to get technical instead of assuming. Let me point out what the raw data shows:

Look at these bars (14:40 to 16:55), all in an uptrend where value keeps rising:

14:30 val_chg +14.04 → state 5
14:40 val_chg +5.39 → state 0 (neutral!)
14:45 val_chg +7.98 → state 5
14:50 val_chg +1.70 → state 0
16:05 val_chg +14.51 → state 5
16:15 val_chg +5.79 → state 0
16:25 val_chg +4.36 → state 5
16:35 val_chg +1.39 → state 0
16:45 val_chg +1.02 → state 5

The state flips between 5 and 0 while the value is CONTINUOUSLY RISING. If state were determined by momentum/slope, a rising line wouldn't drop to neutral and pop back to +5 every other bar. So momentum alone cannot be producing this — my whole "AM sign drives state" assumption is contradicted by these bars. You were right to force this.

But look closer — there's a pattern in a different column. Watch val-close (value minus close) at those flip points:

state 5 bars: val-close = −5.96, +8.77, −2.70, +13.41, +12.07 (mixed, but small/tightening)
state 0 bars right after: val-close = +13.54, +19.72, +20.31, +16.42, +13.60 (consistently larger positive — value well above close)

When value pulls far above the close (val-close large positive), state → 0. When value is near or below close, state → 5. This is a value-vs-price relationship, not momentum. Let me test this precisely across the bars rather than eyeball it.

Ran a command
Ran a command

Important — and it clears the deck of another wrong assumption. value-vs-close does NOT separate the states either. Look: state 0 (neutral) and state 5/6 (committed) have nearly identical vmc medians (NQ up-bars: state 0 = −6.83, state 6 = −6.32 — basically the same). So "how far the line is from price" isn't it. My second guess is also wrong.

But look at the |vchg| column — this is the one that does show a clean pattern, and I dismissed it too early last time:

NQ up-bars: state 0 = |vchg| 2.80, states 4/5/6 = 10.43 / 7.47 / 6.52
NQ down-bars: state 0 = 2.91, states 1/2/3 = 8.43 / 6.65 / 4.06
ES up-bars: state 0 = 0.37, states 4/5/6 = 0.87 / 1.05 / 0.73
ES down-bars: state 0 = 0.41, states 1/2/3 = 1.36 / 0.89 / 0.53

Neutral (state 0) has DRAMATICALLY smaller |vchg| than every committed state — on both instruments, both directions. NQ neutral is 2.8 vs committed 6.5-10; ES neutral is 0.4 vs committed 0.7-1.4. That's a 2-3x gap. So the line's per-bar movement (|vchg|) does strongly separate neutral from committed — it's not that momentum doesn't matter, it's that I was computing it wrong before (I used a smoothed 5-bar slope; the raw 1-bar value change is what separates cleanly).

This resolves the puzzle from the consecutive bars! Remember 14:30→14:40→14:45: value kept rising but state flipped 5→0→5. Look at the |vchg|: those were +14, +5.4, +8. When the 1-bar move was small (+5.4), state went neutral; when it was larger (+8, +14), state went 5. The state tracks the SINGLE-BAR line speed, not the trend. A rising line with a slow bar drops to neutral; the next fast bar pops it back. That's exactly what we saw.

So momentum is the driver — but it's the instantaneous 1-bar line speed, not a smoothed slope. This is actually the ns mechanism from the v5/v6 engines (line_speed = SS − SS[1]), not v7's 5-bar version. Let me nail this precisely: test whether a simple threshold on 1-bar |vchg| (normalized) reproduces the neutral-vs-committed split, and find the threshold from their real state.

Ran a command
Ran a command

Now we have something solid and consistent across both instruments — this is real, technical, and not a guess:

1-bar normalized line speed (ns = |value change| / ATR) separates neutral from committed at 72% on BOTH NQ and ES, with nearly the same threshold (0.12 / 0.14). The medians are cleanly separated: committed ≈ 0.27, neutral ≈ 0.11. That's a 2.5x gap, same on both. So the neutral-vs-committed decision is fundamentally about 1-bar line speed — momentum, but instantaneous, not smoothed.

And there's a partial magnitude signal too: within committed bars, |ns| climbs ±3 (0.20-0.22) → ±5 (0.26-0.30) → ±7 (0.26-0.28) — roughly, though 7 isn't cleanly above 5 on NQ, so speed alone doesn't fully set the magnitude (the LR/V structure does the rest, which we confirmed).

But I have to be honest about the ceiling: 72% is better than v7's mechanism but still not "solved." 28% of bars are misclassified by a pure speed threshold. So there's something else refining the neutral decision beyond raw 1-bar speed. And here's the technical insight about why a fixed threshold caps at 72% — and it connects directly to the word "Adaptive" in Adaptive Momentum:

Look at the overlap: committed 25th percentile (0.165) is below neutral 75th percentile (0.213). There's a zone (0.12-0.22) where the same |ns| is sometimes neutral, sometimes committed. A fixed threshold can't resolve that. But an adaptive one could — if SS360's threshold moves based on recent conditions (e.g. it's harder to commit right after a fresh commit, or the band widens in chop), then the same raw speed gives different states depending on context. That overlap zone is the signature of an adaptive threshold — which is exactly what "Adaptive Momentum" would mean, and it's now evidenced by data, not assumed.

So, technically derived (not guessed):

Neutral-vs-committed = 1-bar line speed vs a threshold (~0.12-0.14 ns) — 72% on both instruments. Confirmed mechanism.
Direction = sign of the 1-bar line change (up=long states, down=short states) — clean.
Magnitude (±3/±5/±7) = LR/V structure (confirmed last session) + partial speed contribution.
The remaining 28% = an adaptive component on the threshold — the "Adaptive" in AM — which we can now hunt specifically, because we've isolated it to this one decision (the overlap zone) rather than the whole state.

This is the technical footing you asked for. We're no longer in the dark on the mechanism — we've derived that it's instantaneous line-speed + LR/V structure, matching their "momentum + structure" description with numbers, and we've pinpointed the one adaptive piece that remains.

The disciplined next step — and this is a specific technical question now, not a guess: what makes the threshold adaptive in that overlap zone? Let me look at the misclassified bars (the ones in the 0.12-0.22 overlap) and find what context flips them — is it time-since-last-commit, prior state, or recent volatility? That's derivable from the data, and it's the last piece.

Want me to isolate the overlap-zone bars and derive what context determines their state — the adaptive component? That's the technical path to closing the 72% → higher, and it's the actual definition of their AM's "adaptive" part.

Claude responded: Yes — exactly that.

Yes — exactly that. Let me be precise about the number, because precision matters here.

What the 72% / 28% actually refers to: it's the accuracy of one specific sub-decision — neutral (state 0) vs committed (state ≠ 0) — using 1-bar line speed alone. On ~740 bars per instrument, a speed threshold correctly calls "is this bar neutral or committed" about 72% of the time, so ~28% of bars get the neutral/committed call wrong by that single rule.

But I want to correct a possible over-read: 28% is NOT our total state mismatch, and it's also not the whole story. Let me give you the honest, complete accounting, because I've been reporting sub-components and you deserve the full picture in one place:

Direction (up/down/neutral): our rule matches theirs well.
Neutral-vs-committed: 72% (the 28% miss we've been discussing) — using speed alone.
Magnitude (±3/±5/±7 once committed): LR/V structure, confirmed.
Full exact-state match (getting the precise 0/±3/±5/±7 right): when I tested v7's complete rule earlier, it was ~53% exact — that's the real bottom-line number for "do we reproduce their exact state per bar," and it's lower than 72% because the 72% is just one of the sub-decisions; errors compound across direction + neutral + magnitude.

So the honest answer to your question: we currently cannot reproduce SS360's exact state on roughly half the bars (53% exact match), and the biggest single source of that miss is the neutral-vs-committed decision, which speed alone gets to 72%.

Now — I owe you a piece of straight fund-manager judgment on top of the number, because I don't want to keep reporting match percentages as if 100% is the goal without questioning whether it is:

Not every bar's state matters equally for trading. Here's the key thing: signals only fire on state transitions into/out of zones (we proved this — 100% signal reproduction from zone crossings). Most bars are mid-leg — sitting in state 5 or 6 for many bars while the move continues. Getting those mid-leg bars slightly wrong (5 vs 6, or a one-bar neutral blip) produces zero signal difference as long as we catch the crossings. So a 53% bar-by-bar state match could still produce, say, 85% correct signals — because the bars we miss are mostly the ones that don't trigger anything.

That's why I keep pushing toward testing signals/P&L rather than bar-match: the 47% of bars we get "wrong" on state may overwhelmingly be trading-irrelevant mid-leg bars. We might already be reproducing their signals far better than our state-match number suggests — and for a better engine, signals and P&L are what count, not per-bar state fidelity.

So before we sink more cycles into the 28%/53%, the honest question is: do the bars we mismatch actually change the signals? That's directly measurable — I can check whether our mismatched-state bars fall on transition bars (which matter) or mid-leg bars (which don't). If they're mostly mid-leg, the state mismatch is largely cosmetic and we should test signals, not chase state. If they're on transitions, then the mismatch genuinely costs us signals and the 28% matters.

Want me to run that — check whether our state-mismatches land on signal-triggering transition bars or on irrelevant mid-leg bars? That single check tells us whether the 28%/53% is a real problem worth solving or a cosmetic one we can bank and move past. It's the disciplined way to decide if the remaining work is even necessary — measured, not assumed.

Now I've got it — and this completely inverts my ±3 model. The structural signatures are crystal clear and consistent:

Look at the direction table — the sign of vchg (line moving up/down) vs where SS sits relative to LR:

+3 SCOUT (state 4): line moving UP (vchg>0, 100%), but SS is BELOW LR (SS>LR = 0%). So the line is rising while still below its LR reference.
−3 WARNING (state 3): line moving DOWN (vchg<0), but SS is ABOVE LR (SS>LR = 100%). Line falling while still above LR.

±3 = the line is moving in one direction but hasn't yet crossed to the confirmed side of LR — it's a COUNTER-position to the LR structure. A +3 is bullish momentum fighting a still-bearish LR position (SS below LR). This is "The Scout" — an early probe against the established structure. My model had the LR condition exactly backwards: I said +3 = "up and SS above LR (weak bull)"; it's actually +3 = "up but SS below LR."

And now the full ladder makes complete structural sense:

+7 (state 6): up, SS>LR AND LR>V — full bull stack (100%/95%). Everything aligned up.
+5 (state 5): up, SS>LR (94%) but LR>V only 38% — SS crossed LR, but the stack isn't complete (LR still below V).
+3 (state 4): up, but SS still below LR (0%) — momentum rising before it's reclaimed LR.

So the magnitude is: how far the rising line has progressed through the LR/V structure. Below LR = +3 (scout), above LR = +5 (base camp), full stack above V = +7 (summit). That's a clean climbing metaphor — exactly matching "Scout → Base Camp → Summit." I had the +3 branch inverted in the Pine.

This is a real, correctable bug in my magnitude logic, derived from ground truth. Let me verify this corrected mapping reproduces their state better before I fix the Pine.

So the real structure: SS360's ±3 "Scout" is a brief probe, not a sustained state. When the line rises below LR, SS360 flashes +3 momentarily then reverts to neutral (0) — it does not stay committed at +3 while below LR. We, on the other hand, keep firing +3 every bar the condition holds. That's the over-firing: we treat "rising below LR" as a sustained +3; they treat it as mostly neutral with an occasional scout flash.

This reframes it correctly, and it aligns with their whole philosophy from the field guide: "below LR" is NOT a confirmed trade state. A move only becomes a real committed state (that they hold) once it crosses LR (+5) — below LR, they're mostly neutral, waiting. The +3 scout is a rare early hint, not a state you sit in.

So the fix is structural and specific: ±3 should be rare/transient, and "rising below LR" should mostly be NEUTRAL, not a held +3. The cleanest version of this: don't commit (leave neutral) until the line has crossed to the confirmed side of LR — i.e., a committed state requires SS past LR (that's +5/+7), and below-LR is neutral except for a genuine fresh-cross scout.

The cost — and this is the honest problem:

Signal match drops: 70%→52% (NQ), 72%→49% (ES). By requiring the LR cross, we now miss more of their actual signals — because some of their real signals fire at the +3 scout stage (below LR), which we're now suppressing.

So it's a precision/recall tradeoff, cleanly exposed: OLD version catches more of their signals but fires tons of junk; LR_REQUIRED fires clean but misses their early (scout-stage) entries. Neither is "right" — the truth is in between, and it tells us something real about SS360:

SS360 fires some signals at +3 (below LR) and some at +5 (crossed LR). It's not purely one or the other. Their +3 is rare but real — an occasional early scout entry — while most of the "rising below LR" bars stay neutral. We can't capture that with a fixed structural rule (either we hold +3 always = over-fire, or never = miss the real scouts). This is exactly the transient nature I found: their +3 flashes occasionally, and which below-LR bar gets the +3 flash is the part we can't yet determine — likely the adaptive-momentum piece again.

The exit dominates the outcome. The entry (theirs vs ours) is a secondary effect on this window.

So exit-independent, our LR_REQUIRED entries look at least as good as SS360's, better on ES — which is genuinely useful because it's not contaminated by the exit. This is the first test that gives our engine a real (if modest) edge on entry quality.

But the ugly part I won't hide — the fav/adv ratio: both engines have fav < adv (0.60-0.93). The adverse excursion exceeds the favorable on both, for both engines. That means after entry, price typically goes against you more than for you before it resolves. That's the choppy-NQ/ES character biting — and it's exactly why the exit matters so much and why take-profit barely broke even. On these two instruments, the raw entry edge is thin because the instruments whipsaw.

And this is precisely your point about the 8 futures. NQ/ES have fav/adv < 1 — they whipsaw, heavy per-bar transactions, mean-reverting chop. Gold, crude, the metals — they may have fav/adv > 1 (trendier, cleaner follow-through), where the same entry logic would show a real distributed edge instead of this thin, exit-dependent result. We genuinely cannot judge the engine on the two worst-behaved instruments. You're right to insist on all 8.

So let me get the 8-futures collection set up. First, what are the 8 tickers? From the vajra doc: 4 indices (ES, NQ, YM, RTY) + 2 metals + 2 energy. Let me confirm the exact ticker symbols SS360 uses, then extend the capture. Let me check the field guide notes and set up the capture for all 8.

Ran a command
Ran a command

The capture tool already loops a TICKERS list — I just need to expand it to all 8 and confirm the exact symbols. I know 4 index (ES, NQ, YM, RTY) and can infer metals/energy, but I shouldn't assume the ticker strings (that's a recurring-mistake flag — don't assume from a label). Let me update the tool to all 8 with the likely symbols, and add a helper so if a symbol is wrong, the tool tells you rather than failing silently.

Ran a command
Ran a command

Let me add one more safeguard — when a ticker returns an empty/malformed response (wrong symbol) rather than an exception, the tool should flag it clearly so you know which symbols to fix. Then hand you the updated version.

Ran a command, read a file
Ran a command, read a file

Done — both tests run, and the 8-futures capture is ready. Here's the honest synthesis.

Test (2) — exit-independent entry quality (the fair entry comparison):
Our LR_REQUIRED entries are as good or better than SS360's own — modestly better on NQ (fav 1.10 vs 0.88), clearly better on ES (fav/adv 0.93 vs 0.75, net@12 +0.38 vs −0.09). This is the first clean read that our entry logic isn't worse — and is somewhat better — once you strip out the exit.

Test (1) + prior — the exit dominates everything:
Reverse-signal exit: both engines lose badly (−110/−76pt NQ). Take-profit: both ~breakeven. The exit swings P&L by hundreds of points; the entry difference is secondary. And the tell-tale: fav/adv < 1 on both NQ and ES — these instruments whipsaw (adverse move exceeds favorable after entry), which is why the raw edge is thin and exit-dependent here.

And that's exactly your point — validated by the data. NQ/ES are the two worst-behaved instruments for this: fast, choppy, mean-reverting, fav/adv below 1. We've been judging the whole engine on the two hardest cases. Metals and energy (GC, SI, CL, NG) have different microstructure — likely trendier, fav/adv possibly above 1 — where the same logic could show a real distributed edge. We can't conclude anything about the engine until we see it across all 8.

The 8-futures capture is ready (ss360_capture_v2.py):

TICKERS now = ES, NQ, YM, RTY, GC, SI, CL, NG (4 indices confirmed, 4 metals/energy best-guess).
I added bad-symbol detection — if any metals/energy symbol is wrong, it prints !! no ripl/ss_line — SYMBOL MAY BE WRONG with the keys it got, so you'll know exactly which to fix (rather than assuming — that's the "don't assume from a label" discipline).

To run it: set your cookie (same as before), then python ss360_capture_v2.py. It'll pull all 8, all timeframes, signals + ssline. Watch for any SYMBOL MAY BE WRONG lines — if GC/SI/CL/NG come back empty, tell me what symbol shows on the SS360 chart for that product and I'll correct it.

Once you've got the 8-futures data collected, the plan is clean: run both engines (SS360's own signals and our LR_REQUIRED) across all 8, with the exit-independent excursion metric first (to see which instruments have fav/adv > 1 — a real entry edge), then the exit work on the instruments that show promise. That's how we find where the edge actually lives, instead of forcing it out of the two choppiest instruments.

One honest flag to set expectations: the metals/energy symbols might not be exactly GC/SI/CL/NG in SS360's system — so the first run might need a symbol correction or two. That's expected; the tool will tell us. Run it and send what comes back (or which symbols it flags), and we'll go from there.

## Claude:
Jul 7


Claude responded: Good hypothesis — and it's exactly the kind of thing that could turn a thin, whipsaw-prone 5m edge into a real one.

Good hypothesis — and it's exactly the kind of thing that could turn a thin, whipsaw-prone 5m edge into a real one. The logic is sound: the field guide's whole philosophy is "ride the Tide" — trade the 5m only when the bigger timeframes agree. If we only take 5m signals when 15m (and/or 1h) point the same way, we should filter out the counter-trend chop that's killing fav/adv on NQ/ES. Let me build that test properly.

First, let me check what higher-timeframe data we actually have, because that determines what alignment I can test cleanly.

Ran a command
Ran a command

Important catch — we only pulled the 5m and tf1 (2m) ssline files to local; the 4h/daily/weekly are in the github repo but not downloaded here. But more fundamentally: we captured ripl(5m), ripl1(2m), ripl4h(4h), daily, weekly — there's no native 15m or 1h in SS360's data. So "15m/1h alignment" can't use SS360's own 15m/1h state (they don't compute those).

That's actually fine — and it points to the cleaner approach. I have two honest options for the alignment test:

Option A — Use SS360's own higher-TF states (4h, daily) that we captured. SS360's ripl4h (4h) and daily are their real regime reads. Align 5m signals to their 4h/daily direction. This uses their ground truth but the frames are 4h/daily, not 15m/1h.

Option B — Compute 15m/1h alignment ourselves from our own engine. Since our TEMA-13 line matches theirs exactly, I can compute our state on 15m and 1h bars (resampling the 5m data) and require the 5m signal to agree with our own 15m (and/or 1h) direction. This gives you exactly the 15m/1h alignment you asked for, self-contained.

Given you specifically said 15m and 1h, Option B is what you want — and it's also the right design for the independent engine (Path B), since it won't depend on pulling SS360's 4h data. Let me build it: compute our state on 5m, 15m, and 1h (by resampling), then test 5m signals filtered by (a) 15m-only alignment and (b) 15m+1h alignment, measuring the exit-independent excursion — the clean metric that isn't muddied by the exit.

Ran a command
Ran a command

This is the strongest signal we've found in the entire project — and your hypothesis was right. Let me read it carefully, because the pattern is dramatic and consistent across both instruments:

As we add higher-timeframe alignment, the entry quality transforms:

NQ:

5m alone: fav/adv 0.60 (whipsaw — adverse exceeds favorable), net@12 −0.03
5m + 15m aligned: fav/adv 1.38 (flips positive!), net@12 +0.05
5m + 15m + 1h aligned: fav/adv 2.25, net@12 +1.12, 72% reach 1 ATR

ES:

5m alone: fav/adv 0.93, net@12 +0.38
5m + 15m aligned: fav/adv 1.56, net@12 +0.72
5m + 15m + 1h aligned: fav/adv 6.56, net@12 +1.45, 90% reach 1 ATR favorable

The alignment filter turns a whipsaw entry (fav/adv < 1) into a genuine directional edge (fav/adv 2-6.5). This is exactly what "ride the Tide" means in their doc, and the data screams it: the 5m edge only exists when the higher timeframes agree. Counter-trend 5m signals are the chop that was killing us; align them and the favorable move dominates the adverse by 2-6x.

Now — I have to be the disciplined fund manager here, because this looks too good and that's precisely when to check the traps:

The honest caveats, stated plainly:

Sample collapses hard. 5m+15m+1h leaves only 11 signals (NQ) / 10 (ES) over 5 days. fav/adv 6.56 on 10 signals is not trustworthy as a number — one or two signals could drive it. This is a strong direction, but the magnitude (especially ES's 6.56) is small-sample and will regress.
5 days, still. The whole caveat stands — this is a hypothesis with a strong signal, not a settled edge.
It's exit-independent excursion, not realized P&L. The favorable move being available doesn't mean an exit captures it. But that's the point of this metric — it says the entry is genuinely better, and now the exit work has something real to capture.

But even with those caveats, this is the most encouraging structural finding we've had, because:

It's consistent across both instruments (both go from fav/adv <1 to >1.3 at 15m, >2 at 1h) — not a one-instrument fluke.
It's directionally monotonic (more alignment → better every step) — that's the signature of a real effect, not noise.
It matches the documented philosophy ("ride the Tide") — mechanism and data agree.

So the honest conclusion: higher-timeframe alignment is very likely a real edge amplifier, and the 15m filter alone (which keeps a usable 23-28 signals with fav/adv ~1.4-1.6) is the sweet spot between edge and sample size. The 1h filter adds more edge but cuts sample too thin to trust yet.

This reshapes the roadmap productively:

The 15m-alignment filter is the highest-value addition to the engine — it's where the entry edge lives, and it converts the thin 5m signal into a directional one.
This desperately needs the 8-futures + more-days data — with only 10-23 signals per instrument, we can't trust the magnitude. On 8 instruments × more days, the 15m-aligned signal set would have enough sample to actually validate.
The exit work now has a real target — on aligned signals where fav/adv is 1.4-2.2, a take-profit or trail can actually capture the favorable move, unlike the whipsaw 5m-alone signals.

My honest recommendation: build the 15m-alignment filter into the engine as the next version (it's the strongest lever we've found), and prioritize the 8-futures + multi-day capture so we can validate it on real sample. The 1h filter is promising but hold it as a second toggle until sample supports it.

Want me to (a) build the HTF-alignment into a new Pine engine version (with 15m and 1h alignment as toggles) so you can run it on TV and we validate on real logs, and/or (b) hold until you've captured the 8-futures data so we test alignment across all instruments with real sample? Given how strong and consistent this is, I lean (a) now — get it into Pine so your TV logs confirm my Python — then (b) validates it broadly. But this is a real finding worth locking into the notes either way.

## Claude:



Claude responded: You're right — and that's a much better idea than computing 15m/1h ourselves.

You're right — and that's a much better idea than computing 15m/1h ourselves. If SS360 computes their own 15m and 1h states, we should pull their ground truth, not approximate it. And your instinct on the keys is likely correct: WAVE and TIDE are the higher-timeframe views (the field guide said TIDE=regime/big-picture, WAVE=medium-swing, RIPL=close-in). So the alignment should use their actual WAVE/TIDE states, not my resampled version.

But I need to be careful and not assume the key names or the timeframes (recurring-mistake flag). Let me reason from what we actually saw in the API structure first.

Ran a command
Ran a command

Here's the honest situation, and it needs a quick check from you rather than me guessing:

What we know: the state object contains ripl_label, wave_label, and tide_label — so SS360 definitely tracks RIPL, WAVE, and TIDE as three views. But the data keys we saw were ripl / ripl1 / ripl4h / daily / weekly. There's no key literally named wave or tide.

So one of two things is true, and I shouldn't assume which:

WAVE and TIDE map to existing keys — e.g., WAVE = ripl4h (4h) and TIDE = daily, OR WAVE = daily and TIDE = weekly. The field guide said RIPL=impulse (close-in), WAVE=medium swing, TIDE=regime (big-picture). If RIPL=5m, then WAVE and TIDE are the higher frames — likely ripl4h/daily or daily/weekly. But I genuinely don't know the exact mapping, and per the vajra doc the futures RIPL itself runs on 2m/5m/10m/30m/65m/195m — so the "15m and 1h" you want might not even be native frames.
WAVE/TIDE are separate keys we didn't see because they only appear under certain conditions, or the request needs a parameter (like &view=wave).

The cleanest way to resolve this — one quick console check from you. Run this on the SS360 chart; it dumps the labels and the actual state values from each timeframe key, so I can see which key's state matches the WAVE label and which matches TIDE (that tells us the mapping definitively, from data, not assumption):

javascript
var _f = window.fetch;
window.fetch = function(){
  return _f.apply(this,arguments).then(function(res){
    if(res.url.includes('vajra/chart')){
      res.clone().json().then(function(d){
        console.log('LABELS:', 'ripl='+d.state.ripl_label, 'wave='+d.state.wave_label, 'tide='+d.state.tide_label);
        // for each TF key, show the LAST bar's state so we can match to labels
        ['ripl','ripl1','ripl4h','daily','weekly'].forEach(function(k){
          if(d[k] && d[k].ss_line && d[k].ss_line.length){
            var last=d[k].ss_line[d[k].ss_line.length-1];
            var gap = d[k].ss_line.length>1 ? (d[k].ss_line[1].time - d[k].ss_line[0].time) : 0;
            console.log(k+': last state='+last.state+', bar-gap='+gap+'s ('+(gap/60)+'min)');
          }
        });
      }).catch(function(e){console.log('err',e)});
    }
    return res;
  });
};
console.log('hook set — switch ticker to trigger');

This prints:

The three labels (ripl/wave/tide) — the current B/S/N read for each view.
For each data key: its last-bar state value and its bar-gap in minutes (so I can confirm which key is which timeframe).

Then I match them: whichever key's state corresponds to the wave_label is WAVE, and whichever matches tide_label is TIDE. That gives us the mapping from data, no guessing — and tells us the actual timeframes (which might be 4h/daily rather than 15m/1h, and that's fine, we use what they actually compute).

Run that and paste the output. Then:

If WAVE/TIDE map to ripl4h/daily (or similar), I update the capture to pull those as the alignment frames, and we re-run the alignment test using SS360's real WAVE/TIDE states instead of my resampled 15m/1h — cleaner and ground-truth.
If it turns out their higher frames are 4h/daily (not 15m/1h), that's actually fine — the alignment test showed the principle works (higher-TF agreement amplifies edge); we just use their actual frames.

One honest note: the alignment finding was strong on my computed 15m/1h. Using SS360's real WAVE/TIDE will be a better test — but the frames may differ from exactly 15m/1h. What matters is that higher-TF alignment helps, and their WAVE/TIDE are the ground-truth higher frames to test it with. Paste the console output and I'll nail the mapping.

Claude responded: Exactly right — switch to the WAVE chart, and the API request it fires will show us the WAVE data structure (and same for TIDE).

Exactly right — switch to the WAVE chart, and the API request it fires will show us the WAVE data structure (and same for TIDE). That's the clean way to see what keys/timeframes those views actually use. Let me give you a capture script that hooks every vajra/chart request and dumps its full structure, so whatever you select — WAVE, TIDE, or a specific timeframe — we see exactly what comes back.

Ran a command, read a file
Ran a command, read a file

Here's the inspector script — wave_tide_inspect.js.

How to use it:

Open the SS360 chart, open DevTools → Console (type allow pasting if it asks).
Paste the whole script, Enter. It arms a hook.
Switch the chart to the WAVE view — that fires a request, and it'll print the structure.
Then switch to the TIDE view — another print.
Optionally switch ticker or timeframe to trigger more.

What it prints for each request:

The URL (in case WAVE/TIDE use a different endpoint or a URL parameter like &view=wave)
The state labels (ripl / wave / tide — the current B/S/N for each)
For every timeframe key with data: bar count, timeframe gap in minutes (so we see exactly which frames exist — 5m, 15m, 1h, etc.), last-bar state, marker count, and field names
A flag for any new key we haven't seen (in case WAVE/TIDE data lives under a key that only appears when you select that view)

What this resolves: whether selecting WAVE/TIDE (a) just changes which existing key is displayed, (b) adds new keys to the response, or (c) uses a different URL. And critically — the bar-gap tells us the real timeframes (whether their WAVE/TIDE are 15m/1h, or 4h/daily, or something else).

Paste the output it prints when you switch to WAVE and TIDE, and I'll:

Map which keys/timeframes are WAVE and TIDE (from the data, not assumption).
Update the capture tool to pull them.
Re-run the alignment test using SS360's real WAVE/TIDE states as the higher-timeframe filter — the ground-truth version of the strong alignment finding we just got.

Claude responded: This is the answer — and it resolves the timeframe mapping definitively, from the data.

This is the answer — and it resolves the timeframe mapping definitively, from the data. Two things I have to correct that we've had wrong this whole time. Let me read it carefully because it matters.

The daily and weekly keys are mislabeled — they are NOT daily and weekly:

daily: gap = 900s = 15 minutes
weekly: gap = 3600s = 60 minutes = 1 hour

So SS360's internal key names are misleading. The actual timeframes are:

ripl = 5m
ripl1 = 2m
ripl4h = 4h
daily = 15m ← this is WAVE
weekly = 1h ← this is TIDE

And the label correlation proves the WAVE/TIDE mapping cleanly. Look at the last NQ fires where labels changed:

When ripl last_state=5 AND daily last_state=5, labels showed ripl=B, wave=B. → the daily key (15m) IS WAVE.
When daily dropped to state=0, wave label flipped to N — confirming daily(15m) = wave_label.
weekly(1h) tracks tide (tide=N throughout, weekly state=0 throughout).

So it's confirmed from the data, not assumption:

WAVE = daily key = 15m
TIDE = weekly key = 1h

This is exactly the 15m and 1h you wanted — and they're already in the data we can capture. We don't need my resampled approximation; SS360 computes real 15m (WAVE) and 1h (TIDE) states, and they're sitting in the daily and weekly keys.

This is a genuinely important correction, so let me record it immediately before anything else (the timeframe mislabeling would burn us later if we forget).
